utterances: Okay.
Thanks very much for the call.
Here's an e-mail.
This is from David, a former military doctor.
And he says I am not surprised by this.
I worked in military medicine for years.
While there are pockets of excellence, it's essentially a huge bureaucracy which offers sub-optimal care.
I doubt that the current frenzy of activity at WRAMC will lead to any long-term changes - that's Walter Reed Army Medical Center.
I'm curious, do your guests see real improvements on the horizon or will the nation forget about this when the Congress moves on to the next big story?
Anne.
Hi, Neal.
Dana just joined us.
Dana Priest also of The Washington Post.
Thanks very much for coming in, Dana.
I'm glad to be here.
And why don't you take that question.
Is this going to mean real improvement, we've seen some actual energy on the part of Congress for the last couple of days?
Well, we really have, and now the president has announced the new commission today, which includes Bob Dole and Donna Shalala.
So yeah, I think so.
It won't be - some of the problems are really long-term fixes.
The VA is such a gigantic system.

How you start to unravel all the bureaucracy is really a hard task, but certainly there's a lot to be done at Walter Reed, some of which they're already doing beyond Building 18, which is the new platoon sergeants that they're putting in place, the new leaders of the units that they have there.
They have brought in a dozen new NCOs to lead those.
So really they are making beyond superficial changes, some structural changes.
What they're not doing so far is - they brought in more non-wounded Army leaders to help out in this regard.
Whether they'll crackdown in a way that will not be beneficial to the patients is unclear.
Because I could see them treating them more like regular Army units, and they really are not.
They really, you know, in many cases have to put their health care first.

Those people you mentioned as the head of the commission appointed by the president, of course, Bob Dole, the former Republican presidential candidate but also a second lieutenant wounded in Italy during the Second World War, a long-time senator from Kansas, of course.
Donna Shalala was a former head of the Department of Health and Human Services under President Clinton, now the president of the University of Miami, I believe.

I have a quick comment in terms of - I'm an active duty public health officer in the Midwest, and I have noticed that we hear a lot about young men and women who die in these various conflicts but we hear very little about the number of wounded.

It's just seems inconsistent for me that at this time of increasing injuries that we seem to be closing and downsizing major medical facilities, including the Great Lakes and Wilfred Hall in San Antonio.
And including Walter Reed Army Medical Center.
Dana Priest, that has played a part, the decision to eliminate this as a military base has played a part in this, has it not?
Well, that's what the Army would like to say.
That because it was on the BRAC list, which is the list of facilities to be closed, is a long decision on their part to do that.
They were going to move them over to the Bethesda Naval and expand that facility.
And some commanders claim that this is a reason they couldn't get more money and more resources, because the buildings were going to be closed to begin with.
I guess my response to that would be, so this was okay, you know, so it was okay that the case workers couldn't manage their cases.
I think if you put it that way, they would say no and they just didn't appeal for the money in the way that they should have.
The other thing that's happening is that the base support systems are being contracted out.
One reason for that is that everybody is deployed.
You don't have any MPs that are available for policing over at Walter Reed; they're all in Iraq.

But the other reason is that there is this general feeling - and this goes back to the Clinton administration - that private industry can do some things better than the government and they should be able to do that.
However, in this case, the transition from the military to the contractor really exacerbated the problem at Building 18.
And the contractor, they either dropped the ball or there's some sort of contracting dispute that delayed the fixing of the building.
I wondered, let me ask you, Anne Hull, this switch from military to civilian contracting, some would blame a lot of the problem on that.
Some would call that an ideological decision.
It's unclear to me what, you know, why that was happening.
It would be hard to call it ideological in the - only in the sense that this trend has been going on since the Clinton administration.
It's not a new idea.
It just reached Walter Reed in the last several years and at a time when they needed all the military bodies they could find for Iraq.
So you have two forces converging there.
Quentin?
Yes.
You're all set?
I appreciate your time.
Thank you.
Thanks very much for the call.
And let me ask you, Dana, you weren't here at the beginning of the broadcast when we were asking Anne Hull how the story got started.
She said you got a phone call from somebody.
What can you tell us about that?
You know, it was someone who had contact with families up there and who had heard some of the stories and couldn't believe what they were hearing, and contacted me.
And we went out for a meal and then I couldn't believe what she was telling me.
And one thing led to another; we made contact with her initial families and then those families led to other families, who led to soldiers and eventually to former and current staff members.
In this sense it was pretty much the typical gumshoe reporting that you do on lots of stories, where people just pass on and you would just burrow deeper and deeper everyday.
We're talking with Dana Priest and Anne Hull, two reporters for the Washington Post who broke the scandal at Walter Reed Army Medical Center.
When we come back from a short break, we'll continue with them.
If you'd like to join us: 800-989-8255, 800-989-TALK.
E-mail us talk@npr.org.

We'll focus not only on the problems they uncovered, but the extent of the problems not just at Walter Reed but throughout the military hospital system, and also about the way the military treated this story once it was reported.
I'm Neal Conan.
You're listening to TALK OF THE NATION from NPR News.
(Soundbite of music)
Today we're talking about the Walter Reeds scandal with the two Washington Post reporters who broke this story, Dana Priest and Anne Hull.

During hearings on the Hill yesterday, Representative Paul Hodes, a Democratic of New Hampshire, grilled Lieutenant General Kevin Kiley, who oversaw Walter Reed until 2004 and is now Army surgeon general.
Is it still your testimony that it wasn't until the Washington Post published accounts that you knew of the failures that occurred at Walter Reed?

By failures at Walter Reed, if you are talking about the individual soldier stories and Building 18 at Walter Reed in the timeframe that was described in the article, I was unaware that those specific cases were going on.
And nothing you had heard up until that point led you to question whether or not you were overseeing a system that was completely dysfunctional and wasn't serving the soldiers?
Lt.
Gen.
KILEY: Well, I - no, sir.
I did not characterize my view of either Walter Reed, the North Atlantic or my other regions as being dysfunctional.
Testimony yesterday during a hearing that was held at Walter Reed Army Medical Center.
We'd like to hear from those of you active duty or veterans this hour who are familiar with the military hospital system.
Give us a call 800-989-8255, 800-989-talk.
E-mail us talk@npr.org.

And let me ask you, Anne Hull, yesterday, finally questions were put to these generals that - sometimes it's difficult as reporters to ask those questions, interesting when they're up there in front of a committee from Congress.
Let me throw my cigarette away.
One woman I spoke to, Melanie Garrett(ph), called this a great getting-up morning.
Right.
We, actually, met with General Weightman before the story published, and that was a good meeting.
He was cordial with us.
And we never met with Kiley, obviously, but it was interesting to watch the heat on these generals yesterday.
And the most, you know, interesting moment was when one of the Congress members asked if they had any words for the Wendell and Annette McLeod.
And General Weightman turned around to face them and apologized, and General Kiley did not immediately do the same thing.
It was General Kiley, I believe, who was, Dana Priest, after the stories began to emerge said, well this is one-sided reporting.
Well, he even went beyond that on that later.
He said it was yellow journalism at its worst.
I think the Army secretary - the defense secretary didn't really agree with them, however, and we saw that Gates asked for the resignation of Harvey the next day and I…
So am I getting it right that the current head of Walter Reeds, General Weightman, was basically fired - it's on his watch and he has accepted responsibility for his part in this.
Then the secretary of the Army who fired him - by doing that put General Kiley back in command - he was fired for not handling his part very well.
I think you've got that right.
Weightman was there for six months.
He had put in place some changes.
He says he still didn't have a clue about how systemic the problems were.
Kiley is Weightman's boss.
He lives on the post.
He was the commander of Walter Reed in 2003 and 2004, when these problems started and really got quite bad.
And he is someone who apologized but then seems to be unclear about what he's apologizing for.
Because as you saw in that exchange, he doesn't actually believe that that problems are as bad as we documented them to be.
And Anne Hull, then the question goes how far up the chain of command does this go not only in the Army, to the chief of staff of the Army, but to the civilian commanders at the Pentagon?

Well, you know, as the three people testified yesterday, they had complained of the chain of command at Walter Reed and got no satisfaction from three generals who had been there since the war started.
And I'm not sure who knew what at the Pentagon.

But, you know, our colleagues who covered the Pentagon have written just yesterday I think it might be - yeah, yesterday's paper - that this is a total, 180-percent-degree change from how Secretary Rumsfeld handled media criticism.
Gates has been not defensive.
He's called for accountability.
He's acknowledged that the media plays a role, and in this case has not tried to back away from anything that our story said.
In fact, he thanked us, which just bowled me over.
It was the opposite during Rumsfeld's era.

If you remember, he criticized the media for reporting out of Iraq that things weren't going so well, and he really never stopped in his determination to try to convince the American public that the media was all wrong and in some cases were unpatriotic.
Let's get some more listeners in on the conversation.
This is Hubbard(ph), Hubbard with us from Lake City in Florida.
Hello?
